coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive detection of protein kinase activity based on the Au NPs mediated electrochemiluminescence amplification of S2O8 2−–O2 system
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jia-Qing
Yu, Lu-Dan
Zhang, Li
Liang, Ru-Ping
Cao, Shu-Ping
Qiu, Jian-Ding
coredata.dc:description: An electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensor based on Au NPs enhanced ECL signal of S2O8 2−–O2 system has been constructed for ultrasensitive detection of protein kinase activity. In the presence of ATP-s and protein kinase A (PKA), Au NPs can be captured on the thoil-phosphorylated peptides modified electrode surface, generating an enhanced ECL emission of S2O8 2−–O2 system. With increasing the PKA activity, more Au NPs can be captured on the modified electrode surface, resulting in the gradually enhanced ECL intensity of S2O8 2−–O2 system. Based on the Au NPs mediated the ECL amplification of S2O8 2−–O2 system, the activity of PKA can be detected sensitively with a limit of detection of 0.0002U/mL, which is much lower than the most sensitive method in previous reports. The good conductivity and high catalytic ability of the Au NPs are revealed to account for the enhanced sensitivity in this ECL biosensor. The ECL biosensor has been successfully used for monitoring PKA activity in biological samples and screening of protein kinase inhibition.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence biosensor
S2O8 2−–O2 system
Protein kinase A
Enhancement effect
Inhibitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: ECL strategy for PKA activity detection
Application of the biosensor in real samples
Stability, reproducibility, and selectivity of the method

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21475056 , 21675078 and 21775065 ), the Landing Project of Science and Technology of Colleges and Universities in Jiangxi Province ( KJLD13010 and KJLD14009 ), the Project of Jiangxi Provincial Department of Education ( GJJ14001 and GJJ14118 ) and the Graduate Student Innovation Special Funds of Nanchang University ( CX2018034 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) CVs and (B) EIS of (a) GCE, (b) CS/GCE, (c) peptide/CS/GCE, (d) phosphorylated peptide/CS/GCE, and (e) Au NPs/phosphorylated peptide/CS/GCE.

ECL-time curves of (a) GCE, (b) CS/GCE, (c) peptide/CS/GCE, (d) phosphorylated peptide/CS/GCE, (e) Au NPs/peptide/CS/GCE, and (f) Au NPs/phosphorylated peptide/CS/GCE in 0.1M Na2S2O8. Scan range: −1.8V ~ 0V.
Effects of (A) peptide concentration, (B) ATP-s concentration and (C) reaction time of PKA.

(A) ECL intensity-time responses of the biosensor to different concentrations of PKA (a - h: 0, 0.0005, 0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, and 0.5U/mL). (B) Relationship between the ECL intensity and logCPKA.

(A) ECL intensity-time curves of the ECL biosensor to ellagic acid (a - h: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, and 8μM). (B) Relationships between the ECL intensity and the concentrations of inhibitors: (a) ellagic acid, (b) DRB, and (c) quercetin. The concentrations of PKA and ATP-s were 0.5U/mL and 100μM, respectively.
Illustration of the ECL biosensor for PKA activity assay.
PKA activity measured in human serum with the ECL biosensor (n =3).

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive detection of protein kinase activity based on the Au NPs mediated electrochemiluminescence amplification of S2O8 2−–O2 system

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Institute for Advanced Study, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Institute for Advanced Study

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Pingxiang University, Pingxiang 337055, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: J.-D. Qiu, College of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensor based on Au NPs enhanced ECL signal of S2O8 2−–O2 system has been constructed for ultrasensitive detection of protein kinase activity. In the presence of ATP-s and protein kinase A (PKA), Au NPs can be captured on the thoil-phosphorylated peptides modified electrode surface, generating an enhanced ECL emission of S2O8 2−–O2 system. With increasing the PKA activity, more Au NPs can be captured on the modified electrode surface, resulting in the gradually enhanced ECL intensity of S2O8 2−–O2 system. Based on the Au NPs mediated the ECL amplification of S2O8 2−–O2 system, the activity of PKA can be detected sensitively with a limit of detection of 0.0002U/mL, which is much lower than the most sensitive method in previous reports. The good conductivity and high catalytic ability of the Au NPs are revealed to account for the enhanced sensitivity in this ECL biosensor. The ECL biosensor has been successfully used for monitoring PKA activity in biological samples and screening of protein kinase inhibition.

ECL biosensor based on the Au NPs mediated ECL amplification of the S2O8 2−–O2 system has been designed for sensitive PKA activity detection and inhibitor screening.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Protein phosphorylation caused by protein kinase plays crucial regulatory role in a majority of biological processes [1]. As a family of enzymes, protein kinase A (PKA) has the simplest structure and the most clear biochemical characteristics. PKA holoenzyme structure consists of two regulatory subunit and catalytic subunit [2,3]. Abnormal expression or overexpression of certain protein kinase activity can lead to diseases such as cancers HIV, diabetes, cardiac diseases and Alzheimer's diseases [4–6]. Obviously, monitoring kinase activity is of great importance and actual meaning. Up to now, various methods have been reported for monitoring kinase activity, including colorimetric [7], radioactive [8], fluorescent [9,10], and electrochemical [11] methods. However, most of them need complex labeled procedures and are difficult to operation. Hence, it is still a challenge to develop simple and sensitive methods for protein kinase assay.

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) refers to the process of forming excited states after electrochemical and chemical reaction on the surface of electrode then emitting light via relaxation [12,13]. In a manner, ECL combines the best of electrochemical and spectroscopic methods to hold both of the wide dynamic range and sensitivity inherited from chemiluminescence and the stability, simplicity and facility of electrochemical methods [14,15]. Based on the above advantages, ECL technique is widely used in DNA analysis [16–18], immunoassay [19–22], environmental detection [23,24], and clinic diagnostics [25]. In recent years, it has been reported that ECL showed potentials in the protein kinase activity assay and inhibitors screening [26–29]. To further improve the sensitivity, Au NPs have been used as carrier or catalyst in designing ECL biosenesors. For example, Li and co-workers developed an ECL biosensor utilizing Au NPs as signal transduction probes to detect kinase activity [30]. Our group constructed an ECL sensor using the biotin-DNA labeled glucose oxidase/AuNPs as multifunctional nanoprobe for analyzing protein kinase activity [31]. However, only few ECL sensors for protein kinase activity assay with high sensitivity were reported [27–31].

Herein, a novel ECL biosensor based on the Au NPs mediated ECL signal amplification of S2O8 2−–O2 system is designed for sensitive assay of protein kinase activity (Scheme 1 ). Au NPs can be assembled on the thoil-phosphorylated peptides modified electrode surface in the presence of PKA and adenosine 5′-[γ-thio] triphosphate tetra‑lithium salt. The assembled Au NPs with good conductivity and highly catalytic activity can mediate and catalyze the ECL reaction of S2O8 2−–O2 system, resulting in an extremely amplified ECL signal for sensitive assay of protein kinase activity and inhibitor screening.

10μL chitosan solution was coated on the cleaned glassy carbon electrode surface and dried in air. The CS/GCE was then dipped into a solution containing 5mM N-ethyl-N′-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide, 8mM N-hydroxysuccinimide and 120μM peptide (H2N-LRRASLGGGGR-OH) for 10h in darkness. The formed peptide/CS/GCE was immersed in a 20mM Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH7.4) containing 20mM MgCl2, PKA and ATP-s for 100min, after washing with pH7.4 PBS, the thoil-phosphorylated peptide/CS/GCE was obtained (Scheme 1).

The thiol-phosphorylated peptide/CS/GCE was dipped into a 50μL Au NPs solution at room temperature for 1h to adsorb Au NPs onto the thiol-phosphorylated peptide/CS/GCE through AuS bond. After washing to remove the nonspecifically adsorbed Au NPs, the obtained Au NPs/phosphorylated peptide/CS/GCE was characterized by ECL technique in a 0.1M PBS buffer solution (pH7.4) containing 0.1M Na2S2O8 at the potential range from −1.8 to 0V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL strategy for PKA activity detection
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Scheme 1, CS, contains a mass of amino, was first coated on the GCE surface. Then, the carboxyl-terminated peptides were coupled on the CS/GCE under the effect of EDC and NHS. In the presence of ATP-s and PKA, the peptides on the peptide/CS/GCE were thiol-phosphorylated. The Au NPs with an average diameter of 15nm were densely captured on the thiol-phosphorylated peptide/CS/GCE through AuS bond (Fig. S1). As a result, Au NPs extremely amplified the ECL signal of S2O8 2−–O2 system because of the good conductivity and excellent catalytic activity of Au NPs towards the ECL reaction of S2O8 2−–O2 system. The amount of Au NPs on the electrode was closely related to the activity of PKA. Therefore, based on the enhancement effect of Au NPs on the ECL signal of S2O8 2−–O2, the activity of PKA can be monitored sensitively using the proposed ECL biosensor.

Cyclic voltametry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were used to investigate the surface features of the ECL biosensor (Fig. 1A). The GCE shows a couple of redox peaks of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (curve a). When a layer of CS was coated on the GCE surface, a tiny decrease of peak current can be observed (curve b). After coupling peptides on CS/GCE surface (curve c) and further thiol-phosphorylating of peptides by PKA and ATP-s (curve d), the redox peak currents reduced obviously and the ΔEp becomes larger. This can be attributed that the replacement of OH by phosphate group increased the negative charge of peptides and hindered electron transfer of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− at the electrode interface. When Au NPs were captured on the thoil-phosphorylated peptide/CS/GCE, the redox peak currents increased (curve e), due to the good conductivity of Au NPs. This phenomenon has also been confirmed by EIS (Fig. 1B). The GCE exhibits low electron-transfer resistance (Ret) (curve a). Coating a layer of CS on GCE led to an increase in Ret due to the poor conductivity of CS (curve b). The Ret further increased after assembly of peptides on CS/GCE (curve c) and phosphorylation of peptides (curve d). An obvious decrease in the Ret value was observed when Au NPs were captured on phosphorylated peptide/CS/GCE (curve e). The EIS results are consistent with the CV results, elucidated that the biosensor has been successfully constructed.

As shown in Fig. 2 , the GCE exhibits low cathodic ECL signal (curve a) in 0.1M Na2S2O8 with the scan range of −1.8V ~ 0V, which was resulted from the dissolved O2 and the coreactant S2O8 2−. The dissolved O2 and the coreactant S2O8 2− would be reduced to O2
− and SO4
− on the electrode surface, respectively. The strong oxidant SO4
− can react with O2
− to generate excited state O2*, which decays to the ground state with light emission (eq. S(1)-S(4)). When CS and peptide were immobilized on the electrode surface step by step and further thoil-phosphorylated in the presence of ATP-s and PKA, the ECL intensities decreased in sequence (curves b-d), due to the poor conductivity of the CS and the peptide. ECL emission changed slightly when incubating peptide/CS/GCE in Au NPs solution (curve e), indicating few nonspecific adsorptions of Au NPs. The capture of Au NPs on the thiol-phosphorylated peptide/CS/GCE through AuS bond generated a strong ECL emission (curve f). This can be ascribed to the good conductivity of Au NPs, which can efficiently facilitate the electron transfer of O2 and S2O8 2− on the electrode surface on one hand. On the other hand, Au NPs can simultaneously catalyze the reduction reactions of O2 and S2O8 2− on the electrode surface to produce more O2
− and SO4
−, respectively. Both O2
− and SO4
− are important species for the formation of excited state O2*; such simultaneously produced O2
− and SO4
− greatly increase the amount of O2*, leading to the effective increase in the ECL signal of the O2. The results confirmed the successful construction of the biosensor, which can be used for PKA activity assay based on Au NPs mediated ECL amplification of the S2O8 2−–O2 system.

The protein kinase activity was evaluated using the proposed ECL biosensor in 0.1M pH7.4 PBS solution containing 0.1M Na2S2O8 at the potential scan range from −1.8V to 0V (Fig. 4 ). As shown in Fig. 4A, the ECL intensities of the S2O8 2−–O2 gradually increased with increasing the concentration of PKA from 0 to 0.5U/mL. As shown in Fig. 4B, the linear range between the ECL intensity and the logarithm of PKA concentration was estimated to be 0.0005 – 0.5U/mL with the correlation coefficient of R2 =0.996. The limit of detection was 0.0002U/mL (S/N=3), which was much lower than those of previous biosensors with other strategies (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the biosensor in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To study the application of the ECL biosensor in real samples, different amount of PKA were added in human serum samples (from the First Affiliated Hospital of Nanchang University). Table 1 shows that the recoveries were in the range of 98–110% for the measurement of 0.01, 0.05 and 0.1U/mL PKA spiked in serum samples with a maximum relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.9%. The results suggested that this strategy based on the Au NPs mediated ECL amplification of the S2O8 2−–O2 system is of great promise in accurate and reliable assay of protein kinase activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, reproducibility, and selectivity of the method
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S2 presents the ECL intensity of the biosensor for 0.5U/mL PKA under 16cycles of continuous potential scan. Stable ECL signal was obtained with RSD of 1.8%, demonstrated the excellent potential cycling stability of the ECL biosensor. Reproducibility of the ECL biosensor was explored by intra-analysis and inter-analysis, respectively. The intra-analysis of the biosensor was evaluated through detecting 0.5U/mL PKA for 5 times, and the inter-analysis of the ECL biosensor was evaluated by detecting the same concentration of PKA with five electrodes. The RSDs of intra-analysis and inter-analysis were 3.2% and 4.3%, respectively, suggesting good repeatability and reproducibility of the ECL biosensor. To study the selectivity of the biosensor, different species including bull serum albumin, glucose oxidase, hemoglobin, casein kinase II, and alkaline phosphatase on the response of the biosensor were tested. The ECL signal remarkably changed only for PKA, while negligible changes in the ECL signal were observed for other species, showing good selectivity of the method.

In summary, a novel ECL biosensor based on the Au NPs mediated ECL amplification of the S2O8 2−–O2 system has been designed for sensitive PKA activity detection and inhibitor screening. The excellent amplification efficiency of Au NPs to the ECL signal of the S2O8 2−–O2 allows the highly sensitive detection of PKA activity with a LOD as low as 0.0002U/mL, which was much lower than those of previous biosensors with other strategies. This can be attributed to the good conductivity and high catalytic activity of the Au NPs captured on the modified electrode suface in the presence of PKA and ATP-s. The proposed ECL biosensor also exhibits excellent performance on PKA inhibitor screening, indicating that this method has potential application in clinical diagnosis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21475056, 21675078 and 21775065), the Landing Project of Science and Technology of Colleges and Universities in Jiangxi Province (KJLD13010 and KJLD14009), the Project of Jiangxi Provincial Department of Education (GJJ14001 and GJJ14118) and the Graduate Student Innovation Special Funds of Nanchang University (CX2018034).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2018.12.018.
